next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

Kai's left ankle clicked when he crouched into the starting blocks, the natural kind, tendon over bone. A fraction later the right one corrected itself. Synthetic muscle tightened to set the joint before he'd finished settling into the pedals. Most days the lag between the two ankles passed beneath notice. This morning it stayed with him, small and stupid, like a word misspelled on a sign he saw too often.

He was in lane three.

Dry air bit low in his throat on the inhale. Two weeks at Kirchberg had been enough for his lungs to stop complaining outright, but morning sessions still left a sting at the bottom of the breath before his chest opened. Six other sprinters moved through warm-ups around the oval. Novak in the far lane, doing strides with a gait so bilaterally perfect it looked unreal. Priya Chandrasekaran near the curve, shaking out her hamstrings, her right leg extending and retracting with the evenness of a fresh calibration. Two athletes Kai knew by face but not by name were jogging easy laps. Behind the glass partition at the hundred-meter mark, two coaches sat with tablets on their knees, watching telemetry.

The start tone sounded, and he came out of the blocks.

The first fifty meters were the part that still felt like running. Feet hit rubber. Knees drove forward. Burn built in his quads with the old familiar honesty of lactic acid and effort and muscle doing exactly what he told it to do. Arms pumped. Breathing locked to footstrikes, short hard exhales. The grafts stayed in the background then, waiting for the point where his body crossed its...
open_hooks_to_carry: Margot plugs into Tomas during hurdle training and pushes his heart rate past the safety margin
Tomas stumbles and falls—minor injury, but the heart-rate spike is flagged in telemetry
Post-feed comedown: the flatness of Margot's own body, her own unplugged senses
prev_tail_excerpt:
...to make him hate it more. Institutional affiliation required. Ethics board approval required. Indemnity coverage required. Archive access billed separately from live-stream access, and live-stream access excluded from the standard license once the new retention model went active. Every line had been written to sound reasonable. Taken together, they meant the same thing as a slammed door.

The incident portal loaded its usual boxes: anomaly class, affected athlete, source dataset, retention authorization. He could fill the first one. Maybe the second, if he picked a name at random and lied by being premature. The third led straight to the files he'd just stolen out from under tomorrow night's switchover. The fourth asked why he'd been keeping other people's raw physiology in a drawer beside his tea.

Formal reporting would make the problem real in exactly the wrong way. Compliance would lock the datasets, Meridian would inherit them, and Yusuf would get whatever filtered summary the company decided a facility engineer was entitled to see. He understood what that made him. The understanding did nothing useful.

The encrypted drive mounted on the laptop. He dragged the day's comparison set into the archive beside older pulls and watched the copy complete. Folders by date. Backups nested inside backups. A private catalogue of bodies that did not belong to him, kept because his sister's nerves were dying and the institutions with the relevant data charged by the gate.

Then he closed the blank incident form, ejected the drive, returned it to the drawer, and turned off the light.
secondary_character_beats: Tomas's fall is minor but the data trail exists; his trust in his coaching team is undamaged, which makes the later betrayal worse

setups_to_plant.description: Margot routinely elevates athletes' heart rates beyond safe margins during feed sessions—the pattern is there in the telemetry data for anyone who reviews it

state_deltas_required: Margot's addiction to the sensory feed—the borrowed intensity of someone else's body
Margot's method visibly costs an athlete physically; she conceals the cause
